frameworks.name: fMRI adaptation
frameworks.one_line: release from adaptation indexes neural discrimination of adjacent stimuli

frameworks.name: categorical perception of color
frameworks.one_line: tests whether early visual cortex shows a category-boundary signature for hue

frameworks.name: continuous hue representation
frameworks.one_line: smooth distance-graded code rather than categorical clusters in early visual areas

core_question: Does early visual cortex represent hue categorically or continuously across the blue-green boundary?
key_assumptions: fMRI adaptation recovery scales with represented distance between stimuli
categorical coding would produce a step-like recovery at the category boundary
early retinotopic areas (V1, V2/3, hV4) carry the relevant hue code
manipulations: hue distance between adjacent stimuli along the blue-green continuum
task: passive viewing versus active categorization
comparison across early visual areas V1, V2/3, hV4
key_findings: release from adaptation increased smoothly with hue distance in V1, V2/3, and hV4
no categorical step in adaptation recovery across the blue-green boundary
direct fMRI response was enhanced for hues near the category border, largest in hV4 and during active categorization
interpretations: early visual cortex carries a noncategorical, continuous hue representation
category-related modulation appears as a response gain near the boundary, especially under task engagement, rather than as a categorical code
